---
title: "Lopsided Indiangrass (Sorghastrum secundum)"
slug: "sorghastrum-secundum"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180850063/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Lopsided Indiangrass"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Poaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Poaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180850063/medium.jpg" alt="Lopsided Indiangrass" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/aclarke2">
aclarke2</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107518663">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Poaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=123822)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

A tall perennial grass characterized by its distinctive one-sided, or “lopsided,” seed heads and narrow leaves. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as flatwoods, savannas, and wet prairies. It grows during the warmer months and contributes to the structure of native grassland communities, especially in fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/123822-Sorghastrum-secundum)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-sorghastrum-secundum/)
